scenario_title: Delete a name that owns only one RRset versus removing the whole name

group_1.behavior: Deleting the last RRset removes the owner name from the namespace, so subsequent queries for leaf.campus.edu. return NXDOMAIN.
group_1.post_state: leaf.campus.edu. is nonexistent; q1 and q2 both observe NXDOMAIN.

group_2.behavior: Deleting the last RRset leaves an empty owner placeholder, so the name remains in use even though it has no RRsets.
group_2.post_state: leaf.campus.edu. exists as an empty node; q1 gets NXRRSET for TXT and q2 gets NXRRSET for A.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4.2 defines delete_rrset, and §3.4.4 defines delete_name, but neither specifies whether removing the final RRset at a non-apex owner must also eliminate the owner name itself from the namespace. RFC 2181 §5.2 discusses RRsets but does not settle the bookkeeping of an empty owner node. As a result, implementations may differ on whether name_in_use remains true after the last RRset is deleted, which is directly visible in follow-up queries and prerequisite evaluation.
expected_divergence.test_rationale: The test creates the smallest possible non-apex name with exactly one RRset, deletes that RRset with delete_rrset, and then queries the same owner for two types. If the server collapses the owner entirely, queries return NXDOMAIN; if it retains an empty placeholder node, the queries return NXRRSET. This exercises the underspecified transition between deleting the last RRset and deleting the name itself, while staying within a single UPDATE followed by read-only observation.
